chapters.title: What Power BI Is and Why It Matters
chapters.summary: Power BI is Microsoft's tool for turning raw data into interactive reports. This chapter sets the stage.

blocks.text: The three parts of Power BI
Power BI has a desktop authoring app, a cloud service for sharing, and mobile apps for viewing.

items: Power BI Desktop: where you build reports.
Power BI Service: where you publish and share.
Mobile apps: view dashboards on the go.

blocks.text: Power BI Desktop is free to download and is where you'll spend most of your learning time.

key_terms.def: A multi-page canvas of visuals built on a data model.
The data and model a report is built on.

exercises: Download Power BI Desktop and open the sample financial dataset.
Identify one chart you could build from it.

links.title: Microsoft Learn: Get started with Power BI

chapters.title: Connecting and Shaping Data with Power Query
chapters.summary: Before you visualize, you clean. Power Query is Power BI's data-prep engine.

blocks.text: Power Query lets you import data and transform it step by step without code.

items: In the Power Query Editor, remove columns you don't need.

key_terms.def: The data-transformation layer used to clean and reshape data.

exercises: Import a CSV and remove two unneeded columns.

chapters.title: What Predictive Modeling Actually Is
chapters.summary: Predictive modeling uses patterns in past data to estimate future or unknown values.

blocks.text: A predictive model learns a relationship between inputs and an outcome, then applies it to new data.
Start by asking: what am I trying to predict, and what data might explain it?

key_terms.def: An input variable used to make a prediction.
The value you are trying to predict.

exercises: List three things a coffee shop might want to predict and the data needed.

chapters.title: What-If Scenario Planning
chapters.summary: What-if planning explores how outcomes change as assumptions change.

blocks.text: Instead of one forecast, you build several based on different assumptions — best case, base case, worst case.

items: Define the key driver (e.g., monthly growth rate).

key_terms.def: A consistent set of assumptions about the future.

exercises: Build a 12-month revenue projection at 3%, 5%, and 8% monthly growth.
